Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty scattered this semester. I tried to set aside time on weekends, but I'd often find myself just going through the motions without really being present. I'd spend maybe 16 hours a week studying, but a lot of that was spread thin across seven different online courses, so nothing got my full attention. I'm a visual learner, so I'd try to organize notes with diagrams and charts, but my motivation was just... low.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due. I'd have these bursts of activity right before deadlines, then long stretches where I barely touched anything. Some weeks I'd be consistent, logging in every other day, but other weeks I'd disappear entirely. It wasn't intentional—life just got in the way, and honestly, I struggled to find the energy to stick to a regular schedule.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline approaching or getting a notification about something new being posted. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if there was anything urgent I'd missed. The gamified tasks actually helped—seeing those progress bars and badges gave me a small push to open the LMS even when I didn't feel like it. But if nothing was pressing, I could go days without thinking about it.
Q4: There were a few weeks mid-semester where logging in became more automatic for me. The gamified elements helped create that habit—I wanted to keep my streak going and see my points accumulate. But that routine was fragile. Once I hit a rough patch emotionally, the habit broke pretty easily, and I had to rebuild it from scratch. It never felt truly ingrained.
Q5: The video lectures were most useful for me since I learn best visually. I watched over two hours worth of content because seeing concepts explained with diagrams and animations made things click in ways reading couldn't. The discussion forums were also helpful when other students shared their visual notes or explained things differently than the professor. Those peer interactions—I had about 36 of them—often clarified things I was confused about.
Q6: I ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. They felt dense and overwhelming, and without visual elements, I'd lose focus quickly. Some optional practice exercises I kept putting off because they didn't count toward my grade and my motivation was already stretched thin. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it had clear, immediate relevance to an assignment or exam. The gamified tasks were good because they gave instant feedback and made progress visible. If something felt abstract or disconnected from what I needed to pass, I struggled to justify spending energy on it. With seven courses to juggle, I had to be selective about where I invested my limited motivation.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones had deadlines and consequences, so they got done—even if barely. Optional stuff, even when interesting, always got pushed aside because there was always something mandatory competing for my attention. The gamified optional tasks were the exception; they felt less like obligations and more like small wins I could collect.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos to my tablet and study them offline, especially when I wanted to avoid the distraction of being online. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations and made handwritten visual notes that I'd review away from my computer. A lot of my actual learning happened with those materials spread out on my desk, not logged into anything.
Q10: There were whole study sessions where I'd work through downloaded content for hours without the LMS registering anything. I'd pause videos, sketch out concepts, and really try to understand things—but none of that showed up anywhere. Also, when I studied with friends or explained things to them, that was valuable learning time that left no digital trace.
Q11: Absolutely. I'd often open a video once, download it or take detailed screenshots, and then never watch it through the LMS again. My video watch time might show two hours, but I probably spent double that reviewing the same content offline. It was easier to focus without the browser tabs and notifications pulling my attention away.
Q12: There were definitely moments when engagement became really hard. I've been dealing with this underlying sadness that makes everything feel heavier than it should. When that hit, even simple tasks felt exhausting. My attendance dropped to almost nothing, and assignments piled up. It wasn't that I didn't care—I just couldn't muster the energy to care enough to act on it.
Q13: All of those things affected me. Juggling seven courses meant I was always behind on something. Fatigue was constant—I'd sit down to study and just feel drained before I even started. The confusion in some modules made me avoid them entirely because figuring out where to begin felt like too much. My extracurricular commitments added another layer of time pressure that made the LMS feel like just one more demand.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd log in, click through some pages, maybe start a video, but my mind would be elsewhere completely. I'd realize I'd been staring at the screen for twenty minutes without absorbing anything. Those sessions technically counted as engagement, but they were hollow. I was physically present but mentally checked out.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even important ones. Part of it was low motivation—I knew they mattered but couldn't make myself start. Part of it was this weird avoidance thing where the longer I waited, the more anxious the task made me, which made me avoid it more. It's a cycle I recognize but struggle to break, especially when I'm feeling down.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the 48 hours before anything was due. Without that pressure, I'd drift. The gamified elements helped a little by creating mini-deadlines and goals, but real deadlines with real consequences were what actually drove my behavior. It's not a healthy pattern, but it's honest.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback on assignments gave me a brief boost, made me feel like maybe I could do this. But when feedback was delayed or critical, it sometimes made me withdraw rather than engage more. With my current emotional state, negative feedback hits harder than it probably should. I'd need time to process before I could use it constructively.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having so much content spread across the LMS made it hard to prioritize. The gamified elements gave some structure to my engagement—clear tasks, visible progress, small rewards. Without those, I might have been even more lost. But the overall structure assumed a level of self-motivation I just didn't have this semester.
Q19: The visual elements and gamification made engagement easier—I could see my progress and feel like I was accomplishing something even on bad days. What made it harder was the sheer volume of content and the lack of clear guidance on what was essential versus supplementary. When everything seems equally important, nothing feels manageable, and I'd freeze up.
Q20: I think that describes me sometimes. High activity could mean someone's logging in, clicking around, trying to figure things out, but not actually completing work because they're overwhelmed or confused. Or they might be using the LMS to access materials they're studying offline. Activity doesn't equal productivity—sometimes it's just anxious checking or aimless browsing when you can't focus.
Q21: That resonates with me too. My exam score was decent despite my low attendance and incomplete assignments because I studied effectively offline. I'd download what I needed and really work through it away from the LMS. Someone looking at my login data might think I was barely trying, but I was putting in hours—just not in ways the system could see.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when someone's active, what they click on, how long they spend on pages. That's useful for spotting students who've completely disengaged. But logs miss the quality of engagement, the emotional state behind the activity, and all the learning that happens offline. They see the shadow of engagement, not the substance of it.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through—it's being present, asking questions in your head, making connections. For me, real engagement is when I'm drawing diagrams, explaining concepts to myself, feeling curious rather than just obligated. That's been rare this semester, honestly.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I've been struggling emotionally, that some days getting out of bed felt like an achievement. It wouldn't show the hours I spent with downloaded materials, the peer study sessions, or the genuine effort I put into the gamified tasks because they were the one thing that made me feel capable. It wouldn't show that I passed despite fighting against my own low motivation every single day.
